comments.author: @WhereonEarth4K
comments.text:
👇 Everything we curated for this Papua New Guinea episode. Books, history, cultural resources, travel essentials.

*📚 BROWSE THE PAPUA NEW GUINEA COLLECTION*
  ◦ Every book, photography volume, and cultural resource we'd recommend for this episode → https://amzn.to/3RMxlSz

*🛍 SEE OUR FULL W.O.E. STOREFRONT*
  ◦ Every destination Collection in one place → https://www.amazon.com/shop/whereonearth

*🎟 TOURS AND EXPERIENCES IN PAPUA NEW GUINEA*
  ◦ Trek the Kokoda Track, cruise the Sepik, or dive Kimbe Bay → https://shorturl.at/9lvUv
  ◦ Browse all Papua New Guinea experiences → https://shorturl.at/Fp3c3

*📖 PLAN YOUR PAPUA NEW GUINEA TRIP*
  ◦ Lonely Planet Papua New Guinea & Solomon Islands, the most comprehensive guide to the country → https://amzn.to/4aeMJxn
  ◦ Lonely Planet Pidgin Phrasebook & Dictionary, covering Tok Pisin and the creoles of Melanesia → https://amzn.to/4vd7thd

*📚 GO DEEPER INTO PAPUA NEW GUINEA*
  ◦ First Contact by Connolly and Anderson, the definitive account of the 1930s Leahy expeditions → https://amzn.to/3PL0vkw
  ◦ The World Until Yesterday by Jared Diamond, drawing on decades of his New Guinea fieldwork → https://amzn.to/4uE4HBO

*🍲 BRING PAPUA NEW GUINEA HOME*
  ◦ Birds of Paradise by Tim Laman and Edwin Scholes, the National Geographic photography landmark → https://amzn.to/4dAwUU3
  ◦ Myth + Magic: Art of the Sepik River by Crispin Howarth, spirit-house carvings from the National Gallery of Australia → https://amzn.to/4wUQpOA

comments.text: About 12 years ago l was in New Guinea surfing at Nusa Island also spend time on the mainland opposite the island...l just wish we had more than a weeks stay to explore more of the mainland found the locals friendly even taking us to explore empty waves. I will be returning one day during my retirement .

comments.text: The women do all the work in PNG. The men just laze about.

comments.author: @harry130747
comments.text:
One of the most crime ridden places on Earth. Go there on independence day when the tribes come together, Witness murder, gunfights and spearfights, get robbed, it's exciting.
The hotels are like POW camps to keep the guests safe.
All of this happened to us, first hand  knowledege.

comments.author: @bliashonglauj9531
comments.text:
What are humans killing each other and conquer other's lands for? Humans think that by doing that,  they would  be famous, became legends, superior, and live forever or what? 

In fact, such actions left  only dark stories behind, and traces of  destroying others' languages and cultures completely forever.  Ex: Why countless Philipinos nowadays carry Spanish last names? Because they were forced or impulsed to change their native  last names to the last names of the  missionaries who converted them into Christianity.  Did God tell missionaries to do that?

comments.author: @AnaLuisaRoqueValdovinos
comments.text: Some of the animals depicted in this documentary are native to Central and South America, including the Turquoise-browed Motmot (Eumomota superciliosa) and Geoffroy’s Spider Monkey (Ateles geoffroyi). Please ensure that proper research is conducted before posting such information.

comments.author: @barry7608
comments.text: Another hodge podge of video clips totally unrelated to the narrative and I suspect much of it not even from New Guinea

comments.author: @billodendaal9225
comments.text: The narration doesn't co-enside with the images of PNG, showing other areas of the world and passing them off to be in PNG is utterly wrong. PNG doesn't have spider monkeys, if fact they don't have any monkeys. Suggest the one that made this documentary go back and research PNG properly with proper PNG images authentic to the island.

comments.author: @gaibarrett752
comments.text: Very disappointed with this documentary.  No PNG people shown at all..shame talking out PNG n showing other ethnic cultures n people. I am from PNG n am sure all PNG people watching this will be clear disappointed.

comments.author: @ruipratas64
comments.text:
This video, in some instances, is an absolute joke.
It shows people that are not native to Papua New Guinea. 
It shows the Philippines Eagle, an Harpy Eagle from South America,  it even shows an hedgehog while speaking about echidnas which was hilarious !

comments.author: @trishaihi3537
comments.text: I'm Papua New Guinean, from the Southern region, Central province. The images of people shown are not Papua New Guinean, the look like Asian, the boats too are not our island canoes and some of the flora and fauna are not native to PNG accept the bogainvillia plant

comments.author: @mummler
comments.text:
All of your documentaries are so full of other cultures and you don't even show the culture you're talking about. I'd like to see the people of Papua New Guinea. But you show people from day of the Dead celebrations, Oriental dancers and native Americans. 
Why don't you try just showing the culture you're talking about instead of mixing in all of these other ones. 
I'm deleting your channel.

comments.text: I stopped watching.  The birds shown as Birds of Paradise were, A macaw from s america, a finch from Africa, a crane from Africa, A Broadbill from Asia and a Pheasant.  Doesn't anybody check these films for accuracy.  I would imagine the whole film is a waste.  I guess anything for views.

@WhereonEarth4K you can´t see much what is going on below the treetop, only a spot free of it where people live, a wekk or more, walking distance away from the next village or town, i growed up in the alps and i love the mountains, i am not a big seaman, so to speak, actualy not at all, anyway when you fly over PNG, Lea. Gurney. Manus, Kavieng, Rabaul, Hagen and so on, when you see the mountains the jungle, the sea you can´t explain you have to see,
if there ever was kinda king kong, he lived in PNG !!!😂

comments.text: I'm a Papua New Guinean 🇵🇬.  While some of the information are not quite accurate, it is disappointing to see images not from the country. Especially the images of the birds of paradise which 32 out of 42 types in the world call the island home.

replies.author: @eddygonzalez2328
replies.text: Well, they did say at the beginning, that it remains vastly undocumented.  I can imagine you have experienced, set foot and walked through trails the rest of the world are yet to see. I've seen other documentaries showing other aspects of life and the people who live down in the cities and those who live in the mountains.  Tribes that belief and chase or kill witches, or people accused of being a witch? Papa New Guinea is truly a unique and fascinating place, specially for archeologist.

comments.author: @flwrangler
comments.text:
No mention of some of the biggest Salt Water crocodiles anywhere, that live in the Fly and Sepik Rivers. They are also home to fresh water New Guinea crocodiles.
If you visit Papua New Guinea. You can visit a tribe deep in rain forest that others call " the crocodile people", who live along the Sepik River. Their children swim near the massive crocodiles, yet the crocs dont eat them!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The Sepik crocodile culture is one of the most extraordinary chapters the film couldn't reach — children swimming among some of the largest saltwater crocodiles on earth because of a bond that goes back generations. Thank you for adding it here. 🌿

comments.author: @Bobskiboy85
comments.text: Why are there so many images and video in this piece that are not from PNG? Even has some video of a Maori marae in NZ when the commentator talks about 'haus tambaran'.  I am English but grew up in PNG for 17 years and travelled all over and speak Motu and Tok Pisin like I do English. For me this is really disrespectful to pass off some images and video as being from PNG.
